text: The surgeon positions the OptiView port at the midline fascia, identifying the peritoneum. A primary 12mm port is placed, followed by an ancillary port in the right upper midline and a subxiphoid port, ensuring placement above the liver.

The surgeon examines the liver, noting its normal size, and confirms the absence of an anterior hiatal hernia. The pylorus is identified, and the surgeon plans to leave approximately six centimeters of antrum from the pylorus, visualizing a vertical line from the lesser curvature. The stomach is decompressed, and dissection begins more than halfway down the greater curvature using a harmonic device, carefully avoiding disruption of the gastroepiploic vessels. The spleen is visible as the surgeon uses a soft grasper to avoid injury while retracting tissue in the upper abdomen.

The surgeon navigates the area with notable vascular supply near the stomach, carefully retracting the stomach upwards to expose the superior aspect. Multiple grasps are used to manage the proximity of the gastroepiploic artery to the stomach, minimizing the risk of bleeding. The stomach is flipped upwards, revealing a thin veil of tissue without vessels, as the dissection progresses towards the hiatus.

The surgeon uses energy devices to manage small vasculature near the stomach, ensuring to stay off the stomach itself. The left cruce is visible, with no hiatal hernia observed. A 34 French bougie is used, and the surgeon employs a powered Echelon stapler with green seam guard buttressing, maintaining a distance of four centimeters from the incisura. The assistant, Tony, carefully grabs at the cut edge to prevent a barbershop pole effect, while the surgeon pulls down to lengthen the stomach.

The surgeon positions the bougie and adjusts the stapler to maintain equidistance from the anterior and posterior aspects of the stomach. The assistant, Tony, holds the cut edge to prevent a barbershop pole effect. The surgeon pulses the stapler, ensuring proper alignment and tension before switching to blue staples.

The surgeon aligns the stapler at the crotch of the stomach, ensuring the correct anterior and posterior distance. Using an Echelon-powered stapler, the surgeon pulses for fifteen seconds at the gastroesophageal junction, while the assistant provides a view under the fundus. The stapler is angled to avoid injury to the hiatus, and the bougie moves easily, indicating proper placement. The stapling creates a broader area at the hiatus and post-hiatus, accommodating the stomach's cardia and allowing space for fullness.

The surgeon examines the staple line of the gastric sleeve, noting its smooth and kink-free appearance. The assistant flips the tip of the sleeve down, ensuring it is tapered for easier removal. The staple line shows excellent integrity without any wavering anteriorly or posteriorly, indicating successful hemostasis and alignment.
